text:
It is now almost half a century since the discovery of the catalytic properties of proton-exchanged zeolites sparked an incredible development of new processes and technologies in the petrochemical industry. Since then, immense efforts have been made to elucidate the atomic-scale events that occur during the catalytic reactions. Progress in the understanding of the relationship between structure, properties and catalytic performance of zeolites has enabled a much more deep insight into the roles of the inherent Brønsted acidity and the structural host-guest geometries that are present in the zeolite channels. In the course of time, our understanding of how the zeolite catalytic function can be controlled has been revolutionized and today the insights from heterogeneous catalysis are closely intertwined with those of molecular chemistry.

This review gives, for the first time, a molecular model of the processes underlying a number of general transformations catalysed by zeolitic Brønsted acid sites, integrating kinetic theory, in situ and operando spectroscopy and computational chemistry. After summarizing the recent developments concerning Brønsted acid sites and zeolite catalysis physics, this review focuses on the catalytic processes involving alkanes, alkenes, aromatics, alcohols and polyhydroxy compounds in which the activation and formation of C–C, C–H and C–O bonds are carried out through different elementary steps. Finally, perspectives are given with respect to understanding zeolite catalysis at the atomic level in order to pave the way towards modeling catalytic mechanisms in detail so as to serve as design tool for the preparation of the desired Brønsted acid catalysts.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
